transcription.text: Good morning Mr. Smith, how are you doing today? I'm doing okay doctor, but I've been having more trouble hearing lately, especially when I'm at restaurants or family gatherings. My wife keeps telling me I have the TV too loud. I understand, and how long has this been going on? I'd say it's been getting worse over the past few years, maybe three or four years now. And do you have any ringing in your ears? Yes, actually, I notice a high-pitched ringing, especially at night when it's quiet. Any dizziness or ear pain? No, nothing like that. Okay, let me take a look at your ears and then we'll do some hearing tests.

segments.text: Good morning Mr. Smith, how are you doing today?
I'm doing okay doctor, but I've been having more trouble hearing lately, especially when I'm at restaurants or family gatherings.
My wife keeps telling me I have the TV too loud.
I understand, and how long has this been going on?
I'd say it's been getting worse over the past few years, maybe three or four years now.
And do you have any ringing in your ears?
Yes, actually, I notice a high-pitched ringing, especially at night when it's quiet.
Okay, let me take a look at your ears and then we'll do some hearing tests.

patient_history: 30 years of occupational noise exposure in manufacturing
Father had hearing aids in his 70s
clinician_observations: Tympanic membranes intact with normal landmarks
Acoustic reflexes present at expected levels
assessments: Mild to moderate degree with sloping high-frequency configuration
Probable etiology: presbycusis and noise-induced hearing loss
audiological_findings: Right ear PTA: 25-60 dB HL (250-8000 Hz)
Left ear PTA: 25-65 dB HL (250-8000 Hz)

clinical_summary.narrative: Mr. John Smith, a 67-year-old male with a history of Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus and hypertension, presented to the audiology clinic reporting progressive hearing difficulty over the past 3-4 years. He describes particular difficulty understanding speech in noisy environments such as restaurants and family gatherings. He also reports occasional high-pitched tinnitus that is most noticeable at night. The patient has a significant history of occupational noise exposure, having worked in manufacturing for 30 years. Comprehensive audiological evaluation revealed bilateral symmetric sensorineural hearing loss of mild to moderate degree with a sloping high-frequency configuration. Speech recognition scores were good at 84% (right) and 80% (left). Tympanometry and acoustic reflexes were normal bilaterally, ruling out middle ear pathology. The clinical picture is consistent with a combination of age-related hearing loss (presbycusis) and noise-induced hearing loss. The patient was counseled on hearing aid options and expressed interest in pursuing amplification with bilateral RIC-style hearing aids featuring directional microphones and tinnitus masking capabilities.

soap_notes.subjective: 67-year-old male presents with chief complaint of progressive hearing difficulty over 3-4 years, particularly in noisy environments. Reports wife notices excessive TV volume. Describes occasional high-pitched tinnitus, worse at night. Denies otalgia, otorrhea, or vertigo. History significant for 30 years occupational noise exposure in manufacturing.
soap_notes.objective: Otoscopy: EAC clear bilaterally, TMs intact with normal landmarks. Audiometry: Bilateral symmetric SNHL, mild-moderate degree, sloping configuration. Right ear: 25-60 dB HL; Left ear: 25-65 dB HL. SRT: R-35 dB, L-40 dB. WRS: R-84%, L-80%. Tympanometry: Type A bilateral. Acoustic reflexes: Present at expected levels bilateral.
soap_notes.assessment:
1. Bilateral symmetric sensorineural hearing loss, mild to moderate, sloping high-frequency configuration
2. Etiology: Mixed presbycusis and noise-induced hearing loss
3. Tinnitus, likely secondary to cochlear damage
4. Good hearing aid candidate based on audiometric profile and motivation
soap_notes.plan:
1. Bilateral RIC hearing aids with directional microphones and noise reduction
2. Custom earmold impressions ordered
3. Tinnitus masking feature to be activated
4. Hearing aid fitting scheduled in 2 weeks
5. 1-month post-fitting follow-up
6. Annual audiometric monitoring recommended
7. Hearing conservation education provided

tasks.title: Schedule Hearing Aid Fitting
tasks.description: Schedule bilateral RIC hearing aid fitting appointment within 2 weeks

tasks.title: Order Custom Earmolds
tasks.description: Process earmold impressions and order custom molds for hearing aid fitting

tasks.title: Schedule 1-Month Follow-up
tasks.description: Schedule post-fitting adjustment appointment for 1 month after initial fitting

tasks.title: Provide Communication Strategies
tasks.description: Prepare and provide patient education materials on communication strategies

tasks.title: Annual Hearing Evaluation Reminder
tasks.description: Set reminder for annual audiometric evaluation
